ontology.foodex2_description: Langsat (Lansium parasiticum) — fresh fruit, FoodEx2 tropical fruit category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for langsat; mapped to tropical fruit category A00LC as closest defensible anchor. Verified against EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for langsat fruit in the international release as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of langsat is approximately 5 to 6 fruits (around 100g of edible flesh), commonly eaten as a fresh snack.

nutrient_source.title: Langsat, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, calcium, and iron sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for langsat (raw). Sugar, dietary fibre, and saturated fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central entry for Lansium parasiticum (FDC ID 168153) where HPB does not report these fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tropical fruits — analogous estimate based on lychee and longan GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for langsat (Lansium parasiticum) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI estimated at approximately 42 (low) based on analogous tropical fruits with similar carbohydrate content and fibre profile: lychee (GI ~57, University of Sydney) and longan (GI ~40–45, peer-reviewed literature). Langsat has 8.2g carbohydrate and ~1.5g fibre per 100g, supporting a low-GI classification. Glycemic load per 100g serving = (42 × 8.2) / 100 = 3.4, rounded to 3.5. This is an estimate; direct GI testing of langsat is recommended for clinical precision.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (12mg/100g) and moderate potassium (~145mg/100g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Langsat is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its very low sodium content (12mg/100g) and moderate potassium (~145mg/100g) support blood pressure management, and a portion of 100–150g contributes negligibly to daily sodium intake while providing potassium consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~42) and moderate carbohydrate (8.2g/100g) with ~1.5g dietary fibre attenuating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Langsat is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (100–150g); its estimated low GI (~42), modest carbohydrate content (8.2g/100g), and ~1.5g dietary fibre help attenuate postprandial blood glucose rise, though individual glycaemic responses to tropical fruits vary and self-monitoring is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, very low sodium, and presence of dietary fibre and polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Langsat poses minimal cardiovascular risk; it contains zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and very low sodium (12mg/100g), while providing dietary fibre (~1.5g/100g), vitamin C (~8mg/100g), and polyphenols that support endothelial health, making it a heart-friendly fresh fruit choice within a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of strong anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, high fibre >3g) limits direct RA benefit despite zero fat and antioxidant presence
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Langsat is not contraindicated in rheumatoid arthritis and provides vitamin C (~8mg/100g) and polyphenols with antioxidant activity, but it lacks omega-3 fatty acids and contains only modest fibre (~1.5g/100g), so it should be paired with omega-3-rich foods and higher-fibre vegetables for comprehensive RA dietary management; moderate portions (100–150g) are advised to limit fructose-driven uric acid production.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Langsat (Lansium parasiticum) is a small, round to oval tropical fruit native to Southeast Asia, widely cultivated in Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, and the Philippines. The fruit grows in clusters on the tree and has a thin, pale yellow to beige skin that is slightly sticky with a milky latex. The translucent white or pale flesh is divided into segments, with a sweet-tart flavour reminiscent of a mild grapefruit or grape, and contains one or more bitter green seeds that are not eaten. Langsat is consumed fresh as a snack and is a popular seasonal fruit at Southeast Asian markets. Nutritionally, langsat is a low-calorie fruit (34 kcal/100g) with a modest carbohydrate content of 8.2g/100g, approximately 6.5g of which is natural sugar, and about 1.5g of dietary fibre. It contains negligible fat (0g/100g) and very low protein (0.4g/100g). Sodium is very low at 12mg/100g, and the fruit provides calcium (10mg/100g), iron (1mg/100g), potassium (~145mg/100g), and vitamin C (~8mg/100g). Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 42 places it in the low-GI category, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The very low sodium and moderate potassium content support blood pressure management consistent with DASH dietary principles. The absence of fat, saturated fat, and cholesterol, combined with antioxidant polyphenols and vitamin C, makes langsat a heart-friendly fruit. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. Langsat is also known as duku, longkong, and lanzones across different Southeast Asian countries, with slight morphological differences between varieties.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of langsat?
Is langsat the same as lanzones?
Can I eat langsat if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Sugar and dietary fibre estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for the same species. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparators (lychee, longan) and is clearly documented as an estimate. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a low-calorie tropical fruit.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; langsat is a whole fresh fruit that naturally contains no fat. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero as this is a whole fresh fruit with no fat content — naturally zero. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 as langsat is a plant food that naturally contains no vitamin B12 or vitamin D — naturally zero. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for langsat not fully reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of April 2026; values are not available from approved sources. sugar_g (~6.5g/100g) and dietary_fibre_g (~1.5g/100g) are estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central data for Lansium parasiticum as HPB does not separately report these fields; these are approximate values. potassium_mg (~145mg/100g) and vitamin_c_mg (~8mg/100g) are estimates from USDA FoodData Central and are consistent with published literature for langsat. GI of ~42 is an analogous estimate based on lychee and longan GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI testing of langsat is recommended for clinical precision. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 used as best available match; FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest tropical fruit category anchor — no dedicated langsat code exists in FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue.
